project_description.text: The Los Angeles Harbor Deepening Project proposes dredging approximately 14.7 million cubic yards of material to deepen the harbor's entrance, main channels, and turning basins to -45 feet MLLW (and -51 feet in some areas) to accommodate larger, deeper-draft ships. Most of the dredged material would be used to build a 190-acre landfill behind a rock dike on the south side of Terminal Island, along with an adjacent shallow-water habitat area, relocation of the Terminal Island Treatment Plant outfall, and utility relocations. Construction was estimated to take about 30 months at a first cost of roughly $59.1 million, using primarily hydraulic cutterhead suction dredges.

affected_community.text: The project is located in the Port of Los Angeles and affects the nearby communities of San Pedro, Wilmington, and Terminal Island, with about 32,746 residents living within 1,000 yards of the harbor. Port-related commerce supports roughly 14,500 direct jobs and $2.56 billion in regional payrolls, and construction would create 100-200 temporary jobs. Other affected groups include commercial shipping interests, port tenants and workers, recreational boaters facing a 6,000-berth shortage in Los Angeles County, sport and commercial fishermen, and nearby schools, recreational facilities, and the Federal Correctional Institution on Terminal Island.

alternatives_overview.text: Alternatives considered included no action, ocean disposal of dredged material, upland/land disposal, a reduced-fill 'environmental quality' plan (about 103 acres), and variations in landfill size and location, with the recommended plan selected for its higher economic benefits. The original landfill was reduced from 282 acres to 190 acres in response to wildlife agency concerns, and the Reeves Field/Seaplane Anchorage area was preserved rather than filled after objections from boating interests. Dredging method alternatives ranged from one large dredge to multiple smaller dredges, with hydraulic cutterhead suction dredges chosen for most of the work.

environmental_impact.text: The project would permanently destroy about 190 acres of soft-bottom benthic and shallow-water habitat, eliminate roughly 150 metric tons of benthic biomass and several tons of fish, and remove about 40 percent of feeding habitat for the endangered California least tern colony on Terminal Island. Short-term impacts include turbidity, reduced dissolved oxygen, possible release of contaminants from sediments, construction noise, and air emissions of hydrocarbons, NOx, SOx, CO, and particulates from dredges, tugboats, and the Catalina quarry. Beneficial effects include long-term water-quality improvement from removal of polluted sediments, a roughly 3:1 gain in water column volume, and a mitigation plan developed with USFWS, NMFS, and CDFG that provides a protected least tern nesting site, a 190-acre shallow-water habitat, and funded least tern studies.

public_response.text: The Draft EIS Supplement/EIR was distributed to about 400 individuals and agencies, and a December 1979 public hearing in San Pedro drew both supporters (stevedoring and shipping interests, the Maritime Administration) and critics. The U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service initially issued a jeopardy opinion and opposed the large fill, NMFS sought stronger mitigation, and California agencies including Fish and Game, the Coastal Commission, and the Department of Navigation and Ocean Development requested more analysis or opposed filling Reeves Field, which was ultimately preserved for small-craft boating. Regional bodies such as SCAG and SCAQMD asked for better treatment of landside, traffic, and air quality impacts, and most concerns were resolved through negotiated mitigation, including protection of least tern nesting sites and creation of shallow-water habitat.

value.name: Proposed Plan (NED Plan)
value.description: The National Economic Development Plan as proposed, involving deepening of Los Angeles Harbor main channel to -45 feet and creation of approximately 190-200 acres of new landfill from dredged spoil, maximizing economic benefits and new land for port development.

value.name: Reduced Fill Area (EQ Plan)
value.description: The Environmental Quality Plan, similar in form to the NED Plan but reducing the area of new landfill to approximately 103-187 acres, resulting in lower environmental impacts on marine life and habitat, lower total project cost, and a somewhat higher benefit-cost ratio, though with reduced total economic benefits.

value.name: Ocean Disposal Plan
value.description: A plan involving complete disposal of all dredged spoil in an unconfined manner approximately 5.8 nautical miles out to sea, with no landfill created; ranked lowest among the three structural alternatives due to lower economic benefits and adverse impacts on the marine environment.

value.name: Land Disposal of Dredged Material
value.description: An alternative involving disposal of dredged material on land rather than in the harbor or ocean, considered but not selected as the preferred plan.

value.name: No Action Alternative
value.description: The no-project alternative, including nonstructural options such as lightering and diversion of cargoes to other ports; found to have generally adverse economic and environmental impacts and determined not to fulfill planning objectives for handling projected commerce at the Port of Los Angeles.

themes.justification: The project centers on deepening harbor channels to accommodate larger vessels (Mobility Networks and Connectivity; Water Infrastructure and Management), while permanently destroying 190 acres of benthic habitat and threatening the endangered California least tern colony (Habitat Conservation and Biodiversity; Infrastructure Impacts on Landscapes).

location.note: Project involves deepening navigation channels and turning basins in Los Angeles Harbor (San Pedro Bay area), with landfill on south side of Terminal Island. Long Beach Harbor is mentioned as context but the project is specifically Los Angeles Harbor.
